sessions.details: recorded 1968-12-12 for Symonds On Sunday. First TX 1968-12-23
recorded 1980-01-23 for Friday Rock Show. First TX 1980-02-15

track_listing.track: Last Of The New Wave Riders
'I May Be Wrong, But I Won't Be Wrong Always'

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well hello there. TV on the radio here, Thomas Vance. And welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
And tonight we have archive sessions by 10 years after. The original line up as they were in 67, 68 and 69.
Also we have the first ever BBC session by that heavy band from the sort of Midlands called Saxon.
And we've got new cuts by Utopia, Kiss, Mike Rutherford, El Shankar and Rexy.
We've got classic cuts by Lone Star, The Tangs, Thin Lizzy and Spirit, among others.
Not forgetting, of course, our Friday Night Connection, which should be good tonight.
But this evening, I think we ought to start with something by Utopia, last of the New Wave Riders.
Their new single, which is called 2000 Man, an old song written by Jagger and Richards of the Rolling Stones years and years ago.
[Music]
[Music]
also to be found on their album Dynasty
and I have in front of me a date sheet
of their European tour
on June 25th they're going to be doing Brighton
June 27th, June 28th, Wembley in London
July 1st, Stafford
and July 3rd, Edinburgh
we played Kiss in answer to quite a number of requests
I've got one here from Neil Kimberley
of Rainford in Merseyside
watcher Neil
one from Eden the Kiss Freak
and one from Peter Johnson
of [redacted address]
Stopsley Luton in Bedfordshire
and finally for Simon Millgate
of Leighton Buzzard in Bedfordshire.
And before that, you heard an ace track
from the Adventures in Utopia album by Utopia.
The track was called The Last of the New Wave Riders,
and here's Lone Star.
[Music]
[Music]
And that was Saxon, and the first of four tonight
in our first ever BBC session by them.
Check, first of five.
Bonus tonight.
Stallions of the Highway, that was.
It's a song, of course, that was kicking around
in the heavy metal charts for quite some time
and was, I believe, their first single.
And before that, you heard Lone Star
and a classic cut from the 77 album Firing On All Six.
The track was Bells of Berlin.
And I played that specially for Terrence Gray
of [redacted address], Lurgan, in County Armagh, Northern Ireland.
Because it seems that he and all his mates
flock into MacMullan's Pub in William Street in Lurgan
and listen to the show on the PA.
Particularly Shane and Paul and Francie and Lemmy and Martin and Yvonne,
whom is loved very much by Terrence.
And have a wee Bushmells, actually.
I'd also like to say hello to somebody by the name of, I think it's Inga Clayton, who is out there in Maplewell near Barnsley.
Very best wishes and lots of love from Steve Graham.
Must also say hello to Rayan Giles and John Collier.
Also Greg Sitsis, that's a tongue twister, who's at Wakefield at the moment.
And finally, we played Lone Star for Dave, Chambo Yaskum Chalmers, Chris Pendleton, Paul Riok and Jim McNally.
And also for Dermot Lyle of [redacted address], Hamilton in Scotland.
back in 1968, ten years after, did this.
Way back in March 1968, they had Alvin Lee on the guitar and vocals,
[Music]
Leo Lyons on the bass, Rick Lee on the drums,
and Chick Churchill on the keyboards,
otherwise known, of course, as Ten Years After.
The Friday Rock Show, it's now 28 and a half minutes after ten o'clock.
I've got to play something for a load of people
who are going round and round and round the Whitchurch airfield.
That is everybody who is running in the National Pedal Car Association 24-hour sponsored race for charity.
All the first-year engineering students at Bristol University, you can pedal nice and softly now to Tangerine Dream.
The solo album he's got out at the moment on Charisma, the album's called Small Creeps Day,
[Music]
and the track you heard there was Overnight Job.
Good luck to those of you who have Genesis tickets, and hard luck to those of you who didn't get them.
I had so many letters from people who said they waited in queues for hours and hours and hours, frequently,
only to be disappointed because the tickets had all been sold out.
So I think the concerts are going to be absolutely magic.
And while we're talking about Genesis,
happy birthday to Steve Hackett and Peter Gabriel
because this week they've had a birthday.
Prior to that you had Tangerine Dream from the 76 album called Stratosphere
and that was the track that we played.
And we played it specially for John Gorn to beckon him in Kent
and he wants me to mention Big Andy
and somebody I think is Hairy Andy
and Pete, the hairy version
and the other Andy and Martin and Richard, also hairy
and Sue, whose fursuit qualities are not discussed in this communication,
and all the dream freaks knocking around the world.
And then John goes on to say,
especially those I haven't yet discovered in the female half of Langley Park's sixth form.
John, do you need a hand?
And now here's Saxon.
[Music]
It's Whitesnake from Love Hunter, the track of the same name,
[Music]
and I played that for Janet Anderson and her mum, dad, and also Vic.
Janet at the moment is in London, in Wimbledon, from the Isle of Wight, and feeling a bit lonely.
Hope it doesn't continue.
Alan Paulston, age 15, of Crawford, Biggar in Lanarkshire, wants me to say hello to Dave and to Julie from the Sunderland Polytechnic.
Also, I've got to say hello to Dave Leighton, I think it is, Tony Hammond, Steve Mullins, and everybody from the Holt School in Huddington.
And I also played that track for Stephen Carlin of Uddington in Glasgow.
And before that, you heard something by Saxon, a track called Motorcycle Man.
Now, last Tuesday I did a gig, definition of gig, disc jockey standing on stage playing records.
I actually did it at Tiffany's, which is in Coventry, which is the home of Two-Tone, and that was really amazing.
It was a Valentine night sort of get-together for everybody from the Henley College of Further Education,
and I had a great night, and I'd like to thank you very much for being so kind to me.
Just to mention a few of the people who gave me bits of paper to read out.
Everybody at ConfTech, Huey, Deborah, Danny, John, Tim, Will, Jackie, Martin, Dub, Mark, Ashton, or possibly Ashow,
sharon and sue people who work at robinson osborne and malls johnny from coventry and also
craig des john jim paul and everybody the henley college engineers are now 10 years after
beautiful that's the song that made him famous recorded in the bbc studios between 67 and 68
that of course was 10 years after alvin lee on the lead guitar playing like a fiend the track
was I'm going home.
Before that, I ain't seen no whiskey.
And the first track you heard was
I feel like going home.
5 past 11 on the Friday Rock Show.
Now, a lot of people have written in
and sort of put PSs on their letter.
You obviously have sussed that I am what's known as a voiceover.
I'm one of those people,
those faceless sort of people that work on commercials,
the voice behind the commercial.
And I also do things in various styles.
Well, I won't tell you what the products are,
but, well, it's a living.
I've been handed here an announcement,
so I thought I'd do this announcement
in a very sort of low-key style.
And this is particularly for the girls, and this is how it goes.
It was St. Valentine's Day yesterday, but did you realize that 1980 is a leap year?
Yes, 1980 is the year when the girls can propose to the fellas.
If there's someone in your life who's been a bit slow, off the mark in popping the question, then let me do it for you.
I'll read out your proposal on Friday the 29th of February.
All you have to do is to let me know your name and address, and his name, and where he lives,
or where you think he's currently staying, and I'll do the rest for you.
So, let Radio 1 pop the question for you on the 29th of February.
Get your card off now.
You can send it direct to me, who? Him, Tommy Vance.
Care of Radio 1, the Friday rock show.
London, W1A4WW.
And I'll do my best to include your proposal.
Who knows? This year, my luck might be in.
Saxon.
Track from a great album. The album is just out on Mercury.
It's by El Shankar.
It's called Touch Me There, and the track you heard was Little Stinker.
It's 13 minutes and a half after 11 o'clock here.
And it's time for the Friday Night Connection.
Now, the tracks we played last week during Flashback 67 were
Me and Bobby McGee by Janis Joplin from the LP Pearl,
All Along the Watchtower by Jimi Hendrix from the guitar album,
that's what we actually played it from,
and On the Road Again by Canned Heat from the LP Boogie with Canned Heat.
And I said it was a two-prong question.
And the answer was, most people got it right, incidentally,
The answer was, all of those people made their name, if you like to put it that way, at the Monterey Pop Festival.
And, excuse me, on the other side of the question was that they, all three of them, died in the latter part of 1970.
Now, unfortunately, we seem to have mislaid the card.
Or, actually, letter.
And, uh, it wasn't me.
It was actually somebody who's not terribly too far from me within the confines of the studio and broadcasting house at all.
Just get on with it.
Thank you very much.
You know who it wasn't me.
It was him.
now i do remember the name of that person but of course if i say the name you're all going to write
in and say i am so and so from such and such an address so what i'd like you to do is to find out
what the connection is between these three bits of music which were put together by that certain
person out there who's a name i do know but address i don't and said individual would you
do me a favor write to me again and claim your 10 pound record token here comes the connection
It was Tony Wilson's fault, not mine.
[Music]
Three tracks, and there is a most definite connection between the three.
If you think you know what it is, write it on a postcard, letter, any form of communication you care to get involved in.
And send it to the Friday Night Connection.
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
and don't forget to put your own friday night connection on the card as well three bits of
music with a theme and the person whose name that i know but i'm not at the moment divulging who put
that one together please identify yourself as soon as possible and we'll dive into the piggy
bank get a few bob for you i.e one 10 pound record token 26 and a half minutes after 11 o'clock have
a listen to the words on this it's a great new song by saxon of course with thin lizzy from live
[Music]
and dangerous the track was still in love with you and it was especially for annette who has
spent seven good months with alan roberts of evington in leicester and also for neil and greg
and richard and steven tim and everybody in the fifth year of greg's school in easley and hans i
think it is and it's also for trevor white who's over there having a holiday in belfast and before
that you heard something that isn't on the saxon album it was by saxon it was called strangers in
On the night, Fish, Calf and Percy of Talbot's Boarding House Bedford School in Bedford happen to like that band.
I've got a gig sheet here of all the gigs that Saxon are going to be doing.
On the 16th of Feb, that's tomorrow, they're doing the West Runston Pavilion in West London,
17th of Feb, Red Car, 19th, Nottingham, 22nd, Newcastle.
Then the rest of the gigs I've got during February and during March as well.
If you'd like a copy of the gig list, just write to me and I'll copy them off and send them to you.
Meister here from Julian Frost of Southlands,
Barrow Road, Burnham-on-Sea and Somerset,
because it was he who won the Friday Night Connection tonight.
Thanks for the phone call, Julian.
You want me to give a mention to everybody at Plymouth Polytechnic?
No problem.
More from ten years after.
in a session they recorded in December 1968
Woman Trouble and Hear Me Calling
and of course now it's ten years later
and a mate of mine's in the band
a gentleman who plays an ace bass
a gentleman by the name of Mick Hawksworth
who lives in the Crystal Palace area of London
Mick, if you're listening, drop round to Broadcasting House
one of these days and I'll buy you a pint of lemonade
Here's a cute little single.
Tell no lies that you gave.
One of them 12 dreams of Dr. Sardonicus in the track was called When I Touch You.
[Music]
And that was especially for Ross, who's a little dog who thinks the show's great.
Because Miss Joanna Kaminsky of 24, I think it is,
Ivy Street, Crossland Moor and Huddersfield
wrote and told me so. Ross, come here, boy.
Woof, woof. Thank you.
Few mentions here for people that I met up in Coventry
on Tuesday. Chris and Des and Dave and John and Craig
T and Jim and Woody and Paul and Tommy.
And also for all the heavy metal friends
up there, including Wes and
Nipper, and for the
Hendy executives, Andy, Tracy, Duncan,
Malcolm, Ian, Teresa, Neil, Mark,
Nicola and Saxon.
Saxon and still fit to
boogie. Bill Byford, Paul
Quinn, Graham Oliver, Steve Dawson
and Frank Gill. Nice first session
for the BBC. And that's it for the
Friday Rock Show this week. Hope you enjoyed Ten Years After
and I hope you enjoyed Saxon. Next week
we have Procol Harum
and Money on
not on Saturday tomorrow. Bill
Bruford, Taggart of Thunderbirds, Orchestral
Maneuvers in the Dark, interviewing and chatting and doing
things and the Ian Hunter Band in concert
between 4 and 7.30. Should be good.
Richard was our engineer tonight. Richard, we
thank you. Our producer was Tony Wilson. Tony
Wilson will now speak. Speak, Tony.
I must not lose the winner's cards, I must not lose the winner's cards, I must not lose the winner's cards.
Oh, he's a good ad-libber, isn't he?
Better than I am.
I'll see you tomorrow at four o'clock.
I'm Thomas Vance in dear old London town.
Wherever you are, take care of yourselves.
Good night, and God bless.
It's midnight, and we're together on all wavelengths, 433, 330, 285, 275, and VHF.
And this is Tim Gudgeon in the Midnight Newsroom.
